title_en: Author Biography and His Biographical Encyclopedia
content_en:
Shams ad-Din Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn Uthman adh-Dhahabi was born in Damascus in 673 AH (1274 CE) into a family of Turkic origin that had settled in Syria. He became the foremost hadith scholar and biographical historian of the fourteenth century CE, producing works of scholarship in biography, hadith criticism, and history that remain essential references in their fields.

Adh-Dhahabi studied under the leading scholars of Damascus, Cairo, Alexandria, and the Hijaz, acquiring an extraordinarily wide network of teachers — he reportedly heard from more than a thousand scholars. Among his most significant teachers was the controversial but brilliant Hanbali scholar Ibn Taymiyyah, with whom he had a complex relationship of intellectual admiration and occasional disagreement.

His magnum opus, Siyar A'lam an-Nubala (Biographies of Noble Figures), is a monumental biographical dictionary of approximately 5,000 entries covering prominent figures in Islamic history from the Companions of the Prophet through the scholars of adh-Dhahabi's own era. Running to approximately twenty-five volumes in modern critical editions, it is one of the largest works of Islamic biographical scholarship ever produced.

Adh-Dhahabi composed Siyar A'lam an-Nubala as a condensed and critically evaluated version of the even larger biographical encyclopedia Tahdhib al-Kamal fi Asma ar-Rijal by his teacher al-Mizzi. While Tahdhib al-Kamal focused specifically on hadith transmitters, Siyar A'lam an-Nubala broadened the scope to include all notable scholars, rulers, and historically significant figures, making it a general history of Islamic civilization through biography.

He died in Damascus in 748 AH (1348 CE), leaving behind a legacy that included not only Siyar A'lam an-Nubala but also Mizan al-I'tidal fi Naqd ar-Rijal (a critical biographical dictionary specifically for weak transmitters), Tarikh al-Islam (a comprehensive chronological history), and dozens of other works. The modern critical edition of Siyar A'lam an-Nubala, produced by Mu'assasat ar-Risalah in Beirut in twenty-five volumes with comprehensive indices, has made adh-Dhahabi's monumental encyclopedia fully accessible to contemporary researchers and students, ensuring that this foundational resource of Islamic biographical scholarship remains a living reference for every generation that engages with the Islamic tradition.
